q: Transition elements are the metals that exist in the block between Groups II and III.
Transition elements are found in Groups 3 to 12 of the Periodic Table.
Iron is used as a catalyst in the manufacture of sulphuric acid.
Copper-nickel (cupronickel) alloys are used in shipbuilding due to their excellent resistance to seawater corrosion.
Gold is used as jewellery because of its beautiful yellow lustre colour and its high reactivity with oxygen and water.
Titanium is a major component in modern jet aircraft and is used in manufacturing artificial joints.
Cobalt is an essential component of vitamin $B_{12}$ in living systems.
IUPAC defines a transition element as a metal whose atom has partially filled $p$ subshells.
A transition element can give rise to cations with incomplete $d$ subshells (orbitals).
The $f$-block lanthanide and actinide series are called the inner transition elements.
The first series $3d$ transition elements run from scandium ($_{21}Sc$) through zinc ($_{30}Zn$).
The third series $5d$ transition elements run from lanthanum ($_{57}La$) through mercury ($_{80}Hg$) except 14 elements of lanthanide series.
The elements Zn, Cd, and Hg are considered transition metals by IUPAC because their $d$ orbitals are partially filled.
The first row of the transition elements is in the fourth row (Period 4) of the Periodic Table.
In the $3d$ series, atomic radii show a gradual increase in their values from scandium to copper.
The decrease in atomic radii occurs because the attraction force between the nucleus and electrons is stronger than the electron-electron repulsion force.
Compared to $s$- and $p$-block elements, the decrease in atomic radii for $3d$ series transition elements is more rapid.
All transition elements in the $3d$ series have quite similar radii, which accounts for their ability to form alloys.
First ionisation energies (IE) of scandium to copper increase slightly as the atomic number increases.
Towards the end of the series, zinc has a smaller atomic size than copper due to the completion of the $d$ orbitals.
Zinc has a larger ionisation energy due to increasing electron-electron repulsion as the occupancy of the $d$ orbitals is completed.
In atoms of transition elements, the $4s$ orbital is slightly higher in energy than the $3d$ orbitals.
In atoms of the transition elements, the $4s$ orbital is normally filled and the rest of the electrons occupy the $3d$ orbitals.
The filling of the $3d$ orbitals generally obeys Hund's rule, with one electron added to each of the five $d$ orbitals before pairing occurs.
Chromium ($_{24}Cr$) and copper ($_{29}Cu$) are the two exceptions to the expected regular filling pattern in the $3d$ series.
For chromium ($_{24}Cr$), the expected electronic configuration is $[Ar] 4s^1 3d^5$.
$3d$ and $4s$ orbitals have nearly the same energy.
The electronic configuration of $_{29}Cu$ is $[Ar] 4s^1 3d^{10}$ because completely filled $3d$ orbitals have extra stability.
In general, the neutral atoms of most transition elements have an essential electronic configuration of $(n-1)d^{1-10} ns^{1-2}$.
In transition metal elements, the $3d$ orbital is the outermost orbital.
When transition metals form ions, the electrons are always removed first from the $4s$ orbital and then from the $3d$ orbitals.
The electronic configuration of the $iron(II)$ ion ($Fe^{2+}$) is $[Ar] 3d^6$.
The electronic configuration of the $iron(III)$ ion ($Fe^{3+}$) is $[Ar] 3d^5$.
The zinc element has completely filled $3d$ orbitals and loses its 2 outer $4s$ electrons to form the $Zn^{2+}$ ion.
Zinc can form compounds in the $+3$ oxidation state because of its stable filled $d$ orbitals.
Copper is considered a transition element because its atom has partially filled $d$ subshells.
Copper is considered a transition element because the $Cu^{2+}$ ion has incomplete $3d$ orbitals.
The essential electronic configuration of the $M^{2+}$ ion of Scandium is $3d^1$.
Transition elements are defined as elements having partially filled $d$ subshells (orbitals).
In a period, the valence shell electronic configuration of transition elements does not change.
Transition elements have the same group similarities as the $s$-block and $p$-block elements.
Transition elements are silvery-white except for copper, which is brownish-red in colour.
Transition elements are generally poor conductors of electricity and heat.
The transition elements have a high density, with iron being 8 times heavier than sodium per cubic centimetre.
Transition elements are significantly more reactive than Group I metals.
Nickel and copper do not react with water or catch fire in the air (oxygen).
In general, transition metals corrode readily in the atmosphere.
Iron combined with non-metal carbon produces an alloy called steel.
Most transition elements form coloured compounds, while Group I and II metals form white compounds.
Transition elements show variable oxidation states because they can only form ions with a $+2$ charge.
Most transition elements can form complex ions.
Compared to Alkali metals, $3d$ series transition metals have low reactivity and do not react vigorously with water.
Transition elements usually lose electrons from the $d$ orbital first, followed by the $s$ orbital.
The $+3$ oxidation states ($M^{3+}$ ions) are more stable at the beginning of the $3d$ series.
Towards the end of the $3d$ series, $+2$ oxidation states ($M^{2+}$ ions) are more stable.
The elements in the middle of the series exhibit fewer oxidation states than those to the left or right.
Iron ($_{26}Fe$) is known to form oxidation states from $+2$ to $+6$.
The highest oxidation state is $+7$ for manganese ($_{25}Mn$).
Ions with the highest oxidation states, like permanganate ($MnO_4^-$), tend to be good oxidising agents.
Vanadium and chromium ions with the lowest oxidation state of $+2$ are good reducing agents.
Divalent ions of $Co^{2+}$, $Ni^{2+}$, $Cu^{2+}$, and $Zn^{2+}$ are excellent reducing agents.
Transition metals usually have their highest oxidation states in compounds with oxygen and fluorine.
The colour of compounds of transition metals is due to the presence of incomplete $d$ orbitals or unpaired electrons.
Transition metal compounds form coloured ions because they transmit part of the visible light and absorb the complement colour.
$Sc^{3+}$ and $Zn^{2+}$ ions are colourless because $Sc^{3+}$ has completely empty $d$ orbitals and $Zn^{2+}$ has completely filled $d$ orbitals.
If the electrons in $(n-1)d$ orbitals are paired, then the substance is a paramagnetic substance.
If the electrons in $(n-1)d$ orbitals are unpaired, then it is a paramagnetic substance.
Copper and zinc are paramagnetic substances.
Scandium, titanium, vanadium, chromium, and manganese are paramagnetic substances.
Substances that are attracted very strongly by the applied magnetic field are said to be ferromagnetic.
Iron, cobalt, and nickel are ferromagnetic substances.
Transition metals are widely used at the industrial level due to their catalytic properties.
Iron is used as a catalyst in the Contact process.
Vanadium(V) oxide ($V_2O_5$) is used as a catalyst in the manufacture of sulphuric acid.
Nickel is used as a catalyst in the hydrogenation of alkenes.
$Titanium(IV)$ chloride ($TiCl_4$) is used for the manufacturing of high-density polyethene.
A complex ion has a metal ion at its centre with a number of other molecules or ions surrounding it.
Paramagnetic substances show weak attraction towards the external magnetic field.
Diamagnetic substances show a strong attraction to the external magnetic field.
Ferromagnetic substances retain their magnetisation for some time even after the external magnetic field is removed.
Titanium is an integral part of modern construction because it is highly corrosion-resistant, strong, and lightweight.
Titanium is used for roofs, flooring, and ceiling decoration in buildings.
Iron is usually mixed with small amounts of other elements to make harder alloy steels with greater tensile strength.
Manganese steel is extremely strong and is used for railway tracks.
Vanadium is used to form alloys of steel with iron.
$Vanadium(V)$ oxide ($V_2O_5$) is used as a catalyst in the ceramics industry.
$Vanadium(V)$ oxide is used as a mordant in the printing and dyeing of fabrics.
Chromium is used to manufacture stainless steel due to its toughness and resistance to heat.
Various chromium salts are the main raw materials in the chemical industry.
Chromium is involved in the production of car brake pads in the automobile industry.
Manganese is used as a catalyst to reduce the octane rating in gasoline.
Manganese is used in dry cell batteries and paint.
Cobalt ion has a brilliant blue colour used as paint, ink, and pigments.
Rechargeable batteries are made up of chromium.
Nickel is used for anti-corrosion coating and nickel plating.
Nickel is primarily used in the production of stainless steel.
Copper is a good conductor of electricity and is used for electrical wire and circuit boards.
Iron is used in modern medicine to treat or prevent anemia.
Copper has been used to treat Wilson’s disease and Menkes disease.
Surgical instruments are made of transition metals such as stainless steel and cobalt-chrome alloys.
Titanium surgical instruments are used because they are lightweight and corrosion-resistant.
Titanium has been used mostly for fabricating artificial hearts.
Titanium is used in artificial hip bone replacements and eyeglass frames.
Gemstones are found in different colours due to the presence of transition metal ions.
The red colour of ruby is due to a trace amount of chromium.
The blue colour of sapphire is due to a trace amount of manganese.
Transition metals like iron, chromium, copper, manganese, and zinc are essential trace elements for human beings.
Copper is used as a fungicide in the form of Bordeaux mixture.
Nichrome wire is an alloy made by mixing chromium and nickel.
